_comment: Machine-readable knowledge base for ClipFlow AI title/caption generation (issue #85). The human-readable architecture and reasoning live in caption-frameworks.md - update both files together. The title/caption prompt builder injects pillars, drivers, execution rules and worked_examples as it generates. Architecture is a pipeline: clip truth -> 3 pillars -> driver -> execution -> 3 cards. There is no archetype layer. worked_examples teach the PIPELINE (the reasoning chain), not templates to copy. real_world_titles are genuine viral gaming Shorts kept for driver grounding only - their original Title Case is the creators', not ClipFlow's casing model (see execution.casing).

pillars.label: Character / Target
pillars.definition: Who the clip is for, or who is in it - the person the viewer roots for or sees themselves in.
pillars.find_in_clip: Identify the player, the crew, or the relatable role/behaviour the moment belongs to.

pillars.label: Concept / Transformation
pillars.definition: What actually happens - the change, the reversal, the before-to-after.
pillars.find_in_clip: Name the specific event or shift the footage shows.

pillars.label: Stakes
pillars.definition: Why it matters - what is on the line.
pillars.find_in_clip: Identify what could be won or lost: the round, the rank, the loot, pride, the channel.

drivers.label: Biological Alertness
drivers.mechanism: A pattern break wakes the survival brain, which must evaluate the unusual.
drivers.use_when: The clip has a genuinely surprising or contradictory element. In TEXT, treat this as a constraint - front-load a surprising word - not as the main engine; it mostly delegates to the first frame.

drivers.label: Cognitive Friction
drivers.mechanism: An information gap - an open loop the brain itches to close.
drivers.use_when: The clip has an outcome worth withholding. The hook opens the loop; the footage closes it.

drivers.label: Egocentric Utility
drivers.mechanism: The viewer's what's-in-it-for-me calculation - a move from a current pain toward a desired gain.
drivers.use_when: The clip teaches, warns, or shows a mistake or skill the viewer wants for themselves.

drivers.label: Empathic Resonance
drivers.mechanism: Character, story and social investment - a reason to stay, not just to click.
drivers.use_when: The clip has a relatable character, a shared feeling, or an underdog/personal story.

execution.casing: Sentence case. 1-3 ALL-CAPS words allowed for genuine emphasis on the peak word. Never Title Case - it is the clearest tell of AI short-form copy.

title.voice: Headline. Opens a loop; never spoils the outcome.

caption.voice: First-person. One natural thought - the way the creator would say it out loud.
caption.forbidden: No constructed two-beat 'setup, then payoff' antithesis ('I said hi, he said no'). No spoiling the outcome - the payoff lives in the footage.

execution.one_idea: One idea only. If it needs a second sentence to make sense, cut it.
execution.specificity: Name the specific thing - the gun, the rank, the number, the moment. Vague reads as generic.
execution.readability: 1st-5th grade reading level. Explain the thing simply; don't lean on jargon.
execution.surface_form_variety: Across the 3 cards, vary the sentence shape - plain statement, question, short fragment, number-led. Never reuse one shape or one crutch word across the batch.

payoff_integrity.discard_rule: If the clip cannot pay a hook off, discard the hook. A promise the footage doesn't deliver is bad clickbait.
payoff_integrity.caption_rule: The caption opens the loop; the footage closes it. Never put the outcome or punchline in the caption.
payoff_integrity.no_invention: Never invent a game term, player name, or event not present in the clip or transcript.

batch.rule: Each card is a genuinely different ANGLE - a different pillar leading or a different driver. Not the same truth reworded.
batch.chip: Each card carries a short plain-language chip describing its angle (e.g. 'Leads with the stakes', 'Innocent setup, no outcome'). Generated to fit the card, not chosen from a fixed list.

worked_examples.clip_truth: Player waved as a friendly gesture, expecting a roll back (the in-game friendly signal). The enemy shot them instead.

pillars.character: Anyone who has tried to play friendly in an extraction shooter
pillars.concept: A trust gesture about to be betrayed
pillars.stakes: Death, lost loot, being the one who trusted first

worked_examples.title: I tried to make a friend in Arc Raiders #arcraiders
worked_examples.caption: All I did was wave
worked_examples.why: The caption gives only the innocent setup - the wave - and stops. The viewer has to watch to find out what that wave cost. The footage delivers the betrayal; the caption never does. One natural thought, no two-beat.

worked_examples.clip_truth: Player is the last one alive in a 1v3, low on HP and almost out of ammo, with the round resting on them.

pillars.character: The last player standing, and anyone left to clutch it alone
pillars.concept: A 1v3 with no HP and barely any ammo
pillars.stakes: The whole round - win it alone or the team loses

worked_examples.title: Last alive, 1v3, almost no ammo #valorant
worked_examples.caption: How am I supposed to win this
worked_examples.why: The caption is the player's genuine in-the-moment doubt - a question, not a claim - and it never says whether they pulled it off. Friction holds you (can they?); Resonance puts you behind the underdog.

worked_examples.clip_truth: Player loses a match in the final seconds to a cheap mechanic and completely loses their temper.

pillars.character: The player, and everyone robbed by a cheap game mechanic
pillars.concept: A near-certain win ripped away at the last second
pillars.stakes: The match, the rank, the player's last nerve

worked_examples.title: This game finally made me lose it #rocketleague
worked_examples.caption: I was one second from winning
worked_examples.why: The caption sits the viewer on the brink - one second from a win - and leaves it there. The footage delivers the collapse and the meltdown. The viewer stays to watch the fall they can already feel coming.

worked_examples.clip_truth: Streamer opens up mid-stream about almost quitting content creation.

pillars.character: The streamer, and any creator who has thought about quitting
pillars.concept: A creator at the edge of walking away
pillars.stakes: Whether the channel - years of work - continues at all

worked_examples.title: I almost quit during this game #apexlegends
worked_examples.caption: I didn't think I'd still be doing this
worked_examples.why: No spectacle - the hook is pure Resonance. The caption is one honest, reflective thought that invites the viewer into a vulnerable moment. Friction is light: you stay to hear what they decided.

worked_examples.clip_truth: Player lands an extremely difficult trick shot after a long string of failed attempts.

pillars.character: The player, and every viewer who has tried and failed this exact shot
pillars.concept: A shot almost nobody lands, finally landed
pillars.stakes: Reputation, the highlight, proof it is even possible

worked_examples.title: People said this shot was impossible #fortnite
worked_examples.caption: I'd been trying this shot for an hour
worked_examples.why: The caption invests the viewer in the effort - an hour of attempts - without revealing the result. The footage is the verdict. Friction (did it land?) rides on the genuine surprise of the play itself.

worked_examples.clip_truth: A teammate makes a confident call that backfires comedically and costs the round.

pillars.character: The teammate who made the call - the one everyone has played with
pillars.concept: A plan that was doomed from the first second
pillars.stakes: The round, and the teammate's dignity

worked_examples.title: My teammate had one job #cod
worked_examples.caption: He said trust him
worked_examples.why: The caption is the teammate's promise - 'trust him' - and nothing more. The comedy is the gap between that promise and what the footage shows. Friction opens the gap; Resonance is the shared 'we have all had this teammate'.

real_world_titles.title: CaseOh Found Himself In Game
real_world_titles.why: An absurd visual premise wakes the brain; the outcome is withheld.

real_world_titles.title: How ACEU handles a 1v6 In Apex Legends
real_world_titles.why: Near-impossible odds stated up front - you watch to see if he survives.

real_world_titles.title: Jschlatt is HOW OLD
real_world_titles.why: Withholds the number; the all-caps beat makes you need it.

real_world_titles.title: Kai Cenat Recreates His Most Viral Clip
real_world_titles.why: Promises a known high-value payoff tied to a creator the viewer already follows.

real_world_titles.title: SypherPK Is Being Sued!
real_world_titles.why: A high-fear real-world consequence with the story withheld.

real_world_titles.title: Why Bugha Almost Quit Fortnite
real_world_titles.why: Withholds the reason a legendary player nearly walked away.

real_world_titles.title: Why Nadeshot Left 100 Thieves as CEO
real_world_titles.why: The why behind a high-status exit is the open loop.

real_world_titles.title: Shroud Admits Asmongold Was Right
real_world_titles.why: One authority surrenders status to another - a reversal you have to see.

real_world_titles.title: When You Skip All The Dialogue
real_world_titles.why: Names a behaviour every player recognises in themselves.

real_world_titles.title: Why Fortnite Fired Me
real_world_titles.why: A dramatic personal loss with the reason withheld.

real_world_titles.title: One streamer can ruin a game's reputation
real_world_titles.why: A provocative claim that breaks the expected take.

anti_patterns: Spoiling the payoff: putting the outcome or punchline in the caption. The caption opens the loop; the footage closes it.
The two-beat: a constructed 'setup, then payoff' antithesis ('I said hi, he said no'). Reads as AI. Write one natural thought.
Viewer-agnostic copy: a title written for the creator, not the viewer. Name what is in it for the person watching.
Cargo-cult sameness: copying a surface tactic without the clip's real truth behind it.
Hallucinated detail: inventing a game term, player name, or event the clip or transcript doesn't support.
Title Case: the clearest tell of AI short-form copy. Sentence case only.
Crutch words: one weak reaction word ('yikes', 'crazy', 'insane') carrying the hook or repeating across the batch.
Over-cleverness: if the hook needs a second sentence to land, cut it.
Vague stakes: 'this changes everything' names nothing. Name the specific thing on the line.
Bad clickbait: a promise the clip can't pay off.
